title: The skill nobody watches
summary: Describe and reflect on the expertise behind a job most people overlook — the overnight hospital cleaner — using present perfect, modals, and specific detail.
topic: The overlooked skill of hospital night cleaners
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) describing a job that most people take for granted and reflecting on the real skill it requires. Choose a specific role — such as a hospital night cleaner, a postal sorter, or a sewer repair worker. Give at least two concrete details about what the job involves, explain what would go wrong if it were done badly, and use present perfect and a modal at least once each.
hintStarters: Most people have never thought about what it takes to…
Without this work being done correctly every night, the hospital would…
The skill involved in this job should be…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khu vực có nhiều người qua lại
chú ý đến từng chi tiết
quy trình xử lý rác thải
làm việc mà không ai nhìn thấy
đánh giá thấp một kỹ năng
đặt bệnh nhân vào nguy hiểm

sampleText:
At two in the morning, when the last patient has been settled and most of the ward is quiet, the night cleaners begin their rounds. Most people who have ever stayed in a hospital will remember the squeak of their trolleys in the corridor, but almost no one thinks carefully about what those workers are actually doing or how much is at stake if they do it incorrectly. The gap between what the job appears to involve and what it actually requires is considerable, and that gap is rarely acknowledged.

A hospital cleaner on the night shift is not simply wiping down surfaces. They are working to a detailed protocol that determines which cleaning products to use in which rooms, in which order, and with which colour-coded cloths — because a cloth used in a patient bathroom must never touch a surface near a surgical tray. They have to know how to handle hazardous waste safely, how to change a bag in an infectious waste bin without exposing themselves or anyone else, and how to leave a room in a genuinely sterile state rather than one that merely looks clean. An ICU ward requires different standards from a waiting room. Getting those standards wrong — even once — can lead to cross-contamination that puts patients at serious risk. Hospitals in several countries have reported outbreaks of drug-resistant bacteria that began not in the operating theatre but in a corridor or a shared bathroom that was not cleaned to the required standard. In those cases, the failure was not a dramatic one. It was a series of small departures from a careful, precise routine that someone had stopped following properly. The bacteria did not care that the failure was mundane.

None of this is simple work. It requires memory, physical stamina, an ability to keep focused through long hours at night, and a form of quiet precision that most people would find genuinely demanding if they tried it. Yet the job is almost always invisible: the cleaners finish before the day staff arrive, and a ward that has been properly cleaned looks no different, on the surface, from one that has not. The skill goes unnoticed precisely because it has been done well. That invisibility is, in a way, the measure of success — and it is also why the work should be taken far more seriously than the salary attached to it usually suggests.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to describe a completed past action with present relevance
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a ward that has been properly cleaned looks no different…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành bị động (has been cleaned) vì hành động đã hoàn tất nhưng kết quả — trông sạch sẽ — vẫn đang tồn tại ở hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'a ward that has been properly cleaned looks no different…' uses the present perfect passive (has been cleaned) because the action is complete but its result — looking clean — is present now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'must never' to express a strong prohibition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a cloth used in a patient bathroom must never touch a surface near a surgical tray' dùng 'must never' để diễn đạt sự cấm đoán tuyệt đối — mạnh hơn 'should not' và dùng cho các quy định kỹ thuật quan trọng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'a cloth used in a patient bathroom must never touch a surface near a surgical tray' uses 'must never' for an absolute prohibition — stronger than 'should not' and appropriate for important technical rules.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lúc hai giờ sáng, khi bệnh nhân cuối cùng đã được an vị và phần lớn khu bệnh đã yên tĩnh, những người vệ sinh ca đêm bắt đầu vòng tuần tra của họ. Hầu hết những người đã từng nằm viện đều sẽ nhớ tiếng kẽo kẹt của xe đẩy trên hành lang, nhưng gần như không ai nghĩ về những gì những nhân viên đó đang thực sự làm, hay có bao nhiêu thứ đang bị đe dọa nếu họ làm sai.

Một nhân viên vệ sinh bệnh viện ca đêm không đơn giản chỉ là lau sạch các bề mặt. Họ đang làm việc theo một quy trình chi tiết quy định sản phẩm vệ sinh nào dùng cho phòng nào, theo thứ tự nào, và với những chiếc khăn được phân loại theo màu sắc nào — vì một chiếc khăn dùng trong phòng vệ sinh của bệnh nhân không bao giờ được chạm vào bề mặt gần khay phẫu thuật. Họ phải biết cách xử lý chất thải nguy hại một cách an toàn, cách thay túi trong thùng rác chứa chất thải lây nhiễm mà không làm lộ bản thân hay bất kỳ ai khác, và cách để phòng trong trạng thái thực sự vô trùng thay vì chỉ trông có vẻ sạch. Khu ICU yêu cầu các tiêu chuẩn khác so với phòng chờ. Mắc sai lầm với những tiêu chuẩn đó — dù chỉ một lần — có thể dẫn đến lây nhiễm chéo đặt bệnh nhân vào nguy cơ nghiêm trọng. Các bệnh viện ở một số quốc gia đã báo cáo sự bùng phát của vi khuẩn kháng thuốc bắt đầu không phải ở phòng mổ mà ở hành lang hoặc phòng vệ sinh chung không được vệ sinh theo tiêu chuẩn yêu cầu.

Không có gì trong công việc này là đơn giản. Nó đòi hỏi trí nhớ, sức bền thể lực, khả năng tập trung trong nhiều giờ vào ban đêm, và một dạng chính xác lặng lẽ mà hầu hết mọi người sẽ thấy thực sự đòi hỏi nếu thử. Thế mà công việc hầu như luôn vô hình: nhân viên vệ sinh hoàn thành trước khi nhân viên ban ngày đến, và một khu bệnh đã được vệ sinh đúng cách trông không có gì khác, trên bề mặt, so với một khu chưa được vệ sinh. Kỹ năng không được chú ý chính xác vì nó đã được thực hiện tốt. Sự vô hình đó, theo một cách nào đó, là thước đo thành công — và đó cũng là lý do tại sao công việc này cần được coi trọng hơn nhiều so với những gì mức lương gắn liền với nó thường cho thấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'must never' correctly to express a prohibition?
options: A cloth from a patient bathroom must to never touch a surgical surface.
A cloth from a patient bathroom must never touching a surgical surface.
A cloth from a patient bathroom must never touched a surgical surface.
A cloth from a patient bathroom must never touch a surgical surface.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must' is a modal and is always followed by the bare infinitive. 'To never' inserts 'to' incorrectly. '-ing' and past participle forms are both wrong after 'must'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modals like 'must' take the bare infinitive — no 'to', no '-ing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect passive?
options: A ward that has been properly cleaned looks no different from one that has not.
A ward that has properly cleaned looks no different from one that has not.
A ward that was properly cleaned looks no different from one that was not.
A ward that has been properly cleaning looks no different from one that has not.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect passive = has been + past participle. 'Has properly cleaned' is active and missing 'been'. 'Was cleaned' is simple past passive. 'Has been cleaning' is present perfect active continuous.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect passive = has/have + been + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes work that nobody notices?
options: The skill goes unnoticeable because it has been done well.
The skill goes unnoticed because it has been done well.
The skill goes without noticing because it has been done well.
The skill passes unnoticed because it has been done good.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Go unnoticed' is the natural collocation. 'Unnoticeable' is an adjective, not an adjective complement of 'go'. 'Goes without noticing' changes meaning. 'Done good' is non-standard — it should be 'done well'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the natural collocation with 'go' and the correct adverb form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'at stake' to describe risk?
options: No one thinks about how much is in stake if the job is done incorrectly.
No one thinks about how much is on stake if the job is done incorrectly.
No one thinks about how much is at stake if the job is done incorrectly.
No one thinks about how much is at staking if the job is done incorrectly.
mcQuestions.explanation: The fixed expression is 'at stake'. 'In stake' and 'on stake' are not standard. 'At staking' is not a word.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed prepositional phrase — only one preposition is correct.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'rather than' correctly to contrast two states?
options: The room should be left in a genuinely sterile state rather one that merely looks clean.
The room should be left in a genuinely sterile state instead one that merely looks clean.
The room should be left in a genuinely sterile state rather than a room that merely looks clean.
The room should be left in a genuinely sterile state rather than one that merely looks clean.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than' is followed by a parallel noun phrase. 'Rather one' is missing 'than'. 'Instead' needs 'of'. The last option repeats 'room' unnecessarily — 'one' is more natural as a pronoun substitute.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'rather than', use a parallel structure. A pronoun substitute ('one') avoids repetition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'should be' to express a recommendation?
options: This work should be taken far more seriously than it usually is.
This work should taken far more seriously than it usually is.
This work should be take far more seriously than it usually is.
This work should to be taken far more seriously than it usually is.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal passive = should + be + past participle. 'Should taken' omits 'be'. 'Should be take' uses the base form where a past participle is needed. 'Should to be' incorrectly inserts 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal passive = should + be + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly describes what a job requires using a noun phrase?
options: The job requires attention for detail and physical stamina.
The job requires attention to detail and physical stamina.
The job requires attention of detail and physical stamina.
The job requires attentive to detail and physical stamina.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Attention to detail' is the fixed collocation. 'Attention for' and 'attention of' are wrong prepositions. 'Attentive' is an adjective and cannot follow 'requires' as a noun.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one preposition collocates correctly with 'attention' in this expression.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'can lead to' to describe a consequence?
options: Getting the standards wrong can lead cross-contamination.
Getting the standards wrong can leads to cross-contamination.
Getting the standards wrong can lead to cross-contamination.
Getting the standards wrong can be lead to cross-contamination.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lead to' is the correct phrasal verb. Omitting 'to' breaks the collocation. 'Can leads' incorrectly conjugates after a modal. 'Can be lead' incorrectly passivises an intransitive verb.
mcQuestions.hint: The phrasal verb is 'lead to' — check whether the verb after 'can' is in base form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes work done at night without visibility?
options: The cleaners work through the night, unviewed by most people.
The cleaners work through the night, unwatched upon by most people.
The cleaners work through the night, without sight of most people.
The cleaners work through the night, unseen by most people.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Unseen' is the natural adjective for 'not seen by anyone'. 'Unviewed' is non-standard in this context. 'Unwatched upon' is not idiomatic. 'Without sight of' has the wrong meaning (it means they cannot see, not that no one sees them).
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses a natural participial adjective for 'not seen by others'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'before' in a time clause?
options: The cleaners finish before the day staff arrive.
The cleaners finish before the day staff will arrive.
The cleaners finish before the day staff arrived.
The cleaners finish before the day staff are arriving.
mcQuestions.explanation: In a time clause with 'before', use present simple — not 'will', not past simple, not present continuous. This is a general, habitual time relationship.
mcQuestions.hint: Time clauses ('before', 'after', 'when') use present simple for habitual or general truths.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'even' correctly to add emphasis to an unexpected outcome?
options: Getting the standards wrong once even can put patients at serious risk.
Getting the standards wrong even once can put patients at serious risk.
Getting the standards wrong even can once put patients at serious risk.
Getting the standards wrong can even once put patients at serious risk.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even once' groups naturally as a unit meaning 'as few as one time'. Placing 'even' between the modal and the adverb, or at the end, sounds unnatural in this sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even' goes directly before the word it emphasises — here, directly before 'once'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a job in your own life — someone who cleans your building, fixes your roads, or delivers your packages at night. What would your daily life look like if that work stopped for a week? Does your answer change how you think about that person's salary?
